request.question: Higher education in Ukraine has a long and rich history, the pioneering research of scholars working in the country’s higher education institutions and academies, such as which Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics?

title: AQAS
Higher education in New Brunswick
Higher Education Commission
Zhukovsky (film)
ANUIES
List of universities and colleges in Denmark
List of universities in Pakistan
Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky
Higher education in Ukraine
List of universities in Australia
sentences: The Agency for Quality Assurance through Accreditation of Study Programs (AQAS) is an independent non-profit organisation dedicated for the accreditation of higher education institutions in Germany.
It is supported by more than 80 member institutions, both higher education institutions (HEIs) as well as academic associations.
AQAS was founded in 2002 and is headquartered in Cologne, North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany.
To date it has accredited more than 3,500 degree programs at universities and universities of applied sciences, including numerous programs outside of Germany.
In January 2009, the German Accreditation Council granted permission to AQAS to accredit quality assurance systems of higher education institutions as well.
Higher education in New Brunswick (also referred to as postsecondary education) refers to education provided by higher education institutions in the Canadian province of New Brunswick.
Higher education has a rich history in New Brunswick.
The first English-language university in Canada was the University of New Brunswick.
Mount Allison University was the first in the British Empire to award a baccalaureate to a woman, Grace Annie Lockhart, B.Sc.
Education is the responsibility of the provinces in Canada and there is no federal ministry governing it.
A Higher Education Commission or Commission on Higher Education is a private or government agency charged with Higher Education.
Commissions responsibilities includes regulating Higher Education, post-secondary education institutions, Universities and other public and private higher education institutions.
The Commission is often headed by the Minister for Education or Commissioner.

Zhukovsky (Russian: Жуковский ) is a 1950 Soviet biopic directed by Vsevolod Pudovkin and Dmitri Vasilyev, based on the life of Russian scientist Nikolai Zhukovsky (1847–1921), founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.
In 1950 Pudovkin received the Best Director award at the 5th Karlovy Vary International Film Festival for this film.
In 1951 Pudovkin, Shebalin, Golovnya, and Belokurov received the Stalin Prize.

The National Association of Universities and Higher Education Institutions (Spanish: "Asociación Nacional de Universidades e Instituciones de Educación Superior", ANUIES) is a non-governmental organization which includes 152 public and private higher education institutions in Mexico.

The association is involved in the development of programs, plans and national policies for higher education, as well as establishing agencies aimed at fostering the development of higher education in the country.
Higher education in Denmark is offered by a range of universities, university colleges, business academies and specialised institutions.

The national higher education system is in accordance with the Bologna process, with bachelor's degrees (first cycle, three years), master's degrees (second cycle, two years) and doctoral degrees (third cycle, three years).

The majority of higher education institutions are the responsibility of the Ministry of Higher Education and Science (Denmark), however, some higher education institutions within the arts are the responsibility of the Ministry of Culture.
Higher education in Pakistan is the systematic process of students continuing their education beyond secondary school, learned societies, and two-year colleges.
The governance of higher education is maintained under the Higher Education Commission (HEC) which oversees the financial funding, research outputs, and teaching quality in the country.
In Pakistan, the higher education system includes the public, private, military, and vocational universities, all accredited by the HEC.

Since independence, new universities have expanded throughout the country with support provided by the University Grants Commission (UGC), which had been an autonomous institution of recognizing universities until 2002 when it was preceded by the HEC.
Pakistan produces about 445,000 university graduates and 10,000 computer science graduates annually.
A number of institutions of higher learning are active in the country, but the HEC recognizes 183 institutions.
This article provides a comprehensive list of higher education institutions active in Pakistan.

Prof Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky (Russian: Никола́й Его́рович Жуко́вский ; January 17 [O.S. January 5] 1847  – March 17, 1921) was a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.
Whereas contemporary scientists scoffed at the idea of human flight, Zhukovsky was the first to undertake the study of airflow.
He is often called the Father of Russian Aviation.
Higher education in Ukraine has a long and rich history.
Its students, graduates and academics have long been known and appreciated worldwide.

The pioneering research of scholars working in the country’s higher education institutions and academies, such as Dmytro Mendeleyev, Mykola Zhukovsky, and Yeugeny Paton, are part of the universal history of scientific progress.
There are 43 universities in Australia: 40 public universities, two international universities, and one private university.

The Commonwealth Higher Education Support Act 2003 sets out three groups of Australian higher education providers: universities, other self-accrediting higher education institutions, and state and territory accredited higher education institutions.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Higher education in Ukraine has a long and rich history, the pioneering research of scholars working in the country’s higher education institutions and academies, such as which Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics?
hop1_docs: Higher education in Ukraine | Higher education in Ukraine has a long and rich history.  Its students, graduates and academics have long been known and appreciated worldwide.  The pioneering research of scholars working in the country’s higher education institutions and academies, such as Dmytro Mendeleyev, Mykola Zhukovsky, and Yeugeny Paton, are part of the universal history of scientific progress.

Russian Academy of Engineering | The Russian Academy of Engineering (RAE) is a public academy of sciences, which unites leading Russian and foreign scientists, engineers, scientific-research organizations, higher educational institutions and enterprises.  Among the full members of the Academy are the persons who enriched the science with outstanding achievements or practically implemented works of high scientific-technical significance in the area of engineering, who made their great personal contribution into organizing one of branches of industry, science, technics and technology.

Institute of Higher Education of NAPS of Ukraine | Institute of Higher Education of NAPS (National Academy of Pedagogical Sciences) of Ukraine - is one of the NAPS Institutes responsible for control, research and development of system of higher education.

Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky | Prof Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky (Russian: Никола́й Его́рович Жуко́вский ; January 17 [O.S. January 5] 1847  – March 17, 1921) was a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.  Whereas contemporary scientists scoffed at the idea of human flight, Zhukovsky was the first to undertake the study of airflow.  He is often called the Father of Russian Aviation.

Moscow State University of Civil Engineering | Moscow State University of Civil Engineering or MGSU (Russian: Московский Государственный Строительный Университет - МГСУ) is a higher education institution located in Moscow, Russia.  MGSU is considered to be flagship of technical science, professional education and advanced research of the civil engineering field within the Russian Federation.  The university holds the status of National Research University which is a federal embodiment for universities aimed to be the core of modernization of science and education sector.

National University Odesa Law Academy | National University "Odesa Law Academy" () is a higher educational institution in Odesa.  The University was founded in 1997 on the basis of Law Institute of Odesa I.I. Mechnikov National University.  Today NU "OLA" is the center of legal education, culture and science of Ukraine, leading higher educational institution of jurisprudence.  According to the State Accreditation Commission of Ministry of Education and Science, Youth and Sports of Ukraine, the University trains specialists in the highest - IV - level of accreditation.

Robert Zemsky | Robert Zemsky (b. 1941) is a Professor of Education, the Chair of the Learning Alliance for Higher Education, and the founding director of the Institute for Research on Higher Education at the University of Pennsylvania Graduate School of Education.  he pioneered the use of prospective student market analyses in higher education.
hop1_titles: Institute of Higher Education of NAPS of Ukraine
Moscow State University of Civil Engineering
response.summary_1:
Higher education in Ukraine — example pioneering scholars (focus: Zhukovsky)

- Passage [1] lists scholars: "Dmytro Mendeleyev, Mykola Zhukovsky, and Yeugeny Paton."
- Passage [4] gives full entry: "Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky" with dates "January 17 [O.S. January 5] 1847 – March 17, 1921" and describes him as "a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics." Also: "He is often called the Father of Russian Aviation."
- Ambiguity/missing facts: name variant ambiguity — "Mykola Zhukovsky" (passage [1]) vs "Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky" (passage [4]); these are candidate variants for the same person and should be reconciled in the next hop if needed.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
  1. No additional retrieval needed (passages already contain the identifying facts for Zhukovsky).
  2. (If verification) fetch "Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky" article — inspect lede/infobox and "Career" or "Biography" sections.
  3. (If needed) search "Mykola Zhukovsky" disambiguation or Ukraine-related pages to confirm Ukrainian name variant.
hop2_docs: Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky | Prof Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky (Russian: Никола́й Его́рович Жуко́вский ; January 17 [O.S. January 5] 1847  – March 17, 1921) was a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.  Whereas contemporary scientists scoffed at the idea of human flight, Zhukovsky was the first to undertake the study of airflow.  He is often called the Father of Russian Aviation.

Nikolay Ivanovich Zhukovsky | Nikolay Ivanovich Zhukovsky (Russian: Николай Иванович Жуковский ; (November 2 [O.S. October 21] 1833 in Ufa – May 10 [O.S. April 28] 1895 ) was a Russian revolutionary and narodnik, a follower of Mikhail Bakunin; he was born in Ufa and died in Geneva.

Nikolay Yegorovich Makarov | Army General Nikolai Yegorovich Makarov (Russian: Никола́й Его́рович Мака́ров ; ] ; born 7 October 1949) was Chief of the General Staff of the Armed Forces of Russia and First Deputy Minister of Defence from 2008 to 2012 when he was replaced by Valery Gerasimov.

TsAGI | The Central Aerohydrodynamic Institute (Russian: "Центра́льный аэрогидродинами́ческий институ́т" , "ЦАГИ", transliterated as "Tsentralniy Aerogidrodinamicheskiy Institut", TsAGI) was founded in Moscow by the pioneer of Russian aviation, Nikolai Yegorovich Zhukovsky on December 1, 1918.
Nikolay Matyukhin | Nikolay Ivanovich Matyukhin (Russian: Николай Иванович Матюхин ; born December 13, 1968 in Zhukovsky) is a Russian race walker.  He is married to the sprinter Tatyana Chebykina.

Nikolay Makovsky | Nikolay Yegorovich Makovsky (Russian: Никола́й Его́рович Мако́вский ; 10 May 1841, Moscow — 18 October 1886, Saint Petersburg) was a Russian painter; one of a quartet of artist siblings that included his brothers Konstantin and Vladimir and his sister Alexandra.

Nikolay Bekryashev | Nikolay Georgiyevich (Yegorovich) Bekryashev (Russian: Никола́й Гео́ргиевич (Его́рович) Бе́кряшев , 1874 – 1939) was a Russian painter.  He was the director of the Museum of the Northern Dvina Culture (currently the Veliky Ustyug Museum of History, Art, and Architecture) from 1924 to 1938 and is credited with the preservation of most of historical and architectural heritage of the town of Veliky Ustyug.
response.summary_2: {"summary":"Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky was a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics. Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky (born January 17 [O.S. January 5], 1847) is often called the Father of Russian Aviation and founded the Central Aerohydrodynamic Institute (TsAGI) in Moscow."}
